content.full_text:
California 
Fair Political 
Practices Commission 
Daphne L. Macklin 
Legislative Advocate 
American Civil Liberties Union 
1127 11th Street, Ste. 602 
Sacramento, CA 
95814 
Dear Ms. Macklin: 
November 19, 1985 
Re: 
Our File No. A-85-235 
This is in response to your letter of October 25, 1985, 
and our telephone conversation of that date, regarding the 
reporting obligations of a lobbyist employer pursuant to the 
campaign disclosure provisions of the Political Reform Act 
(Government Code Sections 81000-91015). 
You stated that your 
employer, the American Civil Liberties Union (ACLU), has made 
or intends to make contributions or expenditures in connection 
with a statewide initiative campaign. 
Government Code Section 82013 defines three types of 
"committees" which must file campaign disclosure statements. 
These include: 
1. 
Recipient Committees--A person or combination of persons 
which receives contributions totaling $500 or more in a 
calendar year; or 
2. 
Expenditure Committees--A person or combination of 
persons which makes "independent expenditures" (see 
Government Code Section 82031) totaling $500 or more 
in a calendar year; or 
3. 
Major Donor Committees--A person or combination of 
persons which makes contributions totaling $10,000 or 
more on a calendar year. 
During our telephone conversation, you stated that the ACLU 
is funded by membership donations and has. made contributions or 
expenditures in connection with elections in prior years. 
You 
also stated that you thought the ACLU has probably filed major 
donor committee reports in the past. 
I advised you that the 
ACLU should probably be filing reports as a recipient committee. 
428 J Street, Suite 800 • P.O. Box 807 • Sacramento CA 95804 .. 0807 • (916)322 .. 5660 


Daphne L. Macklin 
November 19, 1985 
Page 2 
This advice is based on the language of FPPC regulation 2 Cal. 
Adm. Code Section 82015 (copy enclosed), which states in part: 
"Contribution" includes any monetary or nonmonetary 
payment for which full and adequate consideration 
is not made to the donor that is received by any 
person or organization other than a candidate, con-
trolled committee, official committee of a political 
party, or organization formed or existing primarily 
for political purposes if the payment is "earmarked" 
for the making of contributions or expenditures. 
A 
payment is "earmarked" when, at the time of making 
the payment, the donor knows or has reason to know 
that the payment or funds with which the payment will 
be commingled will be used to make contributions ... 
or expenditures •... 
Because the ACLU has made contributions in previous years, 
its members "have reason to know" that some or all of their 
donations may be used for political purposes. 
Therefore, the 
funds used to support or oppose candidates or measures are 
"contributions" received by the ACLU. 
Enclosed are the manuals and forms you will need to begin 
filing as a recipient committee. 
In addition, you should review 
the activities of the ACLU to determine if recipient committee 
statements should have been filed during previous years. 
Please 
do not hesitate to contact me if I can be of any further assistance. 
Sincerely, 
Carla Wardlow 
Political Reform Consultant 
Enclosures 


October 25, 1985 
Consultant, Technical Compliance 
Fair Political Practices Commission 
P.O. Box 807 
1100 K St. 
Sacramento, 
CA 
95804 
Dear Sir or Madam: 
The American Civil Liberties Union Affiliates of Northern and Southern 
California jointly operate a legislative office here in Sacramento. 
On behalf 
of my employers, and at their request, I would like your response to the following 
questions: 
1. 
What rules apply with regard to reporting and disclosure requirements 
for employers of loybbyists when that employer is contributing or 
participating in a state-wide initiative campaign? 
2. 
What are the appropriate forms and deadlines for their completion and 
submission by such an employer? 
I would appreciate a response in writing with appropriate references to 
any relevant statutes, regulations or guidelines. 
I would also like to discuss 
these questions by telephone if possible at your earliest convience. 
cc: Dorothy Ehrlich, Executive Director, ACLU-NC 
Ramona Ripston, Executive Director, ACLU -SC 
Respectfully, 
Daphne L. Macklin 
Legislative Advocate
